title: Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
pseudobibeR.fr provides tools for multidimensional analysis (MDA) of French register variation based on BiberŌĆÖs (1988) feature framework. The package extracts 66 linguistic features from parsed French text and performs factor analysis to identify underlying dimensions of variation across text types (registers).


MDA reveals systematic patterns of linguistic co-occurrence that characterize different registers (spoken conversation, academic writing, news, fiction, etc.). Rather than analyzing features in isolation, MDA identifies dimensions - groups of features that vary together because they serve similar communicative functions.
For example, personal pronouns, contractions, and present tense tend to co-occur in interactive, spoken registers, while nominalizations, prepositions, and longer words cluster in informational, written registers.

title: Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
Choosing a Parser: UDPipe vs.┬ĀspaCy
Before extracting features, you need to parse your French text using a dependency parser. pseudobibeR.fr supports both UDPipe and spaCy.

Our Recommendation: UDPipe
We recommend using UDPipe unless you have specific reasons to use spaCy.
Why UDPipe?

Simplicity: Pure R implementation with no Python dependencies
Installation: Single command (udpipe::udpipe_download_model("french"))
Reliability: No reticulate/Python environment complications
Performance: Slightly higher dimensional R┬▓ (0.715 vs 0.703)
Factor structure: Cleaner separation of perfect aspect and passive constructions

When to use spaCy:

YouŌĆÖre already using spaCy in your analysis pipeline
You need spaCyŌĆÖs additional NLP capabilities (named entity recognition, etc.)
You prefer spaCyŌĆÖs parsing for other reasons

Both parsers are scientifically valid: Our cross-parser validation demonstrates that UDPipe and spaCy produce nearly identical MDA results (R┬▓ difference of only 0.012), with the same factor structure and register rankings. You can confidently choose based on technical convenience rather than validity concerns.

title: Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
Basic Workflow

Step 1: Parse Your Text with UDPipe

library(udpipe)

# Download French model (only needed once)
udpipe_download_model(language = "french")

# Load the model
french_model <- udpipe_load_model("french-gsd-ud-2.5-191206.udpipe")

# Your corpus as a data frame with doc_id and text columns
corpus <- data.frame(
  doc_id = c("doc1", "doc2", "doc3"),
  text = c(
    "Bonjour! Comment allez-vous aujourd'hui?",
    "Les r├®sultats de l'analyse d├®montrent une corr├®lation significative.",
    "Marie m'a dit qu'elle viendrait demain."
  ),
  register = c("spoken", "academic", "fiction")
)

# Parse the corpus
parsed <- udpipe_annotate(
  french_model,
  x = corpus$text,
  doc_id = corpus$doc_id
) |>
  as.data.frame()



Step 2: Extract Biber Features

# Extract all 66 features, normalized per 1,000 words
features <- biber(parsed, measure = "none", normalize = TRUE)

# Join with register labels
features <- features |>
  left_join(corpus |> select(doc_id, register), by = "doc_id") |>
  select(doc_id, register, everything()) |>
  mutate(register = as.factor(register))

# View feature counts
head(features)



Step 3: Perform Multidimensional Analysis

# Extract factor loadings (3 factors, loading threshold 0.35)
loadings <- mda_loadings(
  obs_by_group = features,
  n_factors = 3,
  cor_min = 0.2,
  threshold = 0.35
)

# View the loadings table
attr(loadings, 'loadings')

# Create visualizations
screeplot_mda(features)                    # Eigenvalues
stickplot_mda(loadings, n_factor = 1)      # Register positions on Factor 1
heatmap_mda(loadings, n_factor = 1)        # Register clustering



Step 4: Statistical Analysis

# ANOVA: Test for register differences
f_aov <- aov(Factor1 ~ group, data = loadings)
summary(f_aov)

# Linear regression
f_lm <- lm(Factor1 ~ group, data = loadings)
summary(f_lm)

# Post-hoc comparisons
TukeyHSD(f_aov)

title: Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
Understanding the Output

Feature Extraction
The biber() function returns a data frame with:

doc_id: Document identifier
f_01_past_tense through f_67_neg_analytic: 66 feature counts
Normalized to per 1,000 words (when normalize = TRUE)



Factor Loadings
The mda_loadings() function performs:

Correlation matrix: Computes correlations between features (minimum cor_min)
Factor analysis: Extracts n_factors using principal components
Rotation: Varimax rotation for interpretability
Loading matrix: Features ├Ś Factors with loadings > threshold

The output includes:

loadings: Factor loading matrix (features as rows, factors as columns)
Individual documents with factor scores in columns Factor1, Factor2, etc.
group: Register labels for visualization



Interpreting Factor 1
In French (like English), Factor 1 typically represents Interactional vs.┬ĀInformation Production:
Positive pole (Interactional): - Personal pronouns (je, tu, il) - Questions, discourse particles - Contractions (pŌĆÖtit, mŌĆÖsieur) - Clausal coordination - Registers: Spoken, fiction (dialogue)
Negative pole (Informational): - Nouns, nominalizations - Prepositions, long words - Passives, participles - Registers: Academic, news, Wikipedia

title: Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
Working with Your Own Data

Data Structure Requirements
Your parsed data frame must include these columns:

doc_id: Unique document identifier
token: Word token
lemma: Lemmatized form
upos (UDPipe) or pos (spaCy): Universal POS tag
dep_rel: Dependency relation
head_token_id: Head token ID



Handling NA Values
Some features may produce NA for very short documents. Use replace_nas():

features <- replace_nas(features, replace_with = 0)



Filtering Registers
For MDA, consider:

Minimum document length: Exclude very short texts (< 50 words)
Register balance: Ensure adequate representation
Outliers: Check for extreme feature values (normalization artifacts)

Getting Started with pseudobibeR.fr
text:
Alternative Parser: Using spaCy
If you prefer spaCy:

library(reticulate)
library(spacyr)

# Initialize spaCy (only once per session)
spacy_initialize(model = "fr_core_news_lg")

# Parse corpus
parsed_spacy <- spacy_parse(
  corpus$text,
  doc_id = corpus$doc_id,
  dependency = TRUE,
  lemma = TRUE,
  pos = TRUE
)

# Extract features (same workflow)
features_spacy <- biber(parsed_spacy, measure = "none", normalize = TRUE)

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
This vignette demonstrates the robustness of multidimensional analysis (MDA) for French register variation by comparing results across two different NLP parsers: spaCy and UDPipe. Despite different parsing algorithms and training data, both models produce remarkably consistent dimensional structures.
We also compare the French dimensional structure to BiberŌĆÖs (1988) original English dimensions, showing cross-linguistic alignment in the fundamental distinction between Interactional and Information Production registers.






NoteData



This vignette uses the French Register Corpus (1.4M tokens, 6 registers). For detailed corpus composition and token counts by register, see Data Sources.

Despite different parsing algorithms and training data, both models produce remarkably consistent dimensional structures.
We also compare the French dimensional structure to BiberŌĆÖs (1988) original English dimensions, showing cross-linguistic alignment in the fundamental distinction between Interactional and Information Production registers.






NoteData



This vignette uses the French Register Corpus (1.4M tokens, 6 registers). For detailed corpus composition and token counts by register, see Data Sources.

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Cross-Parser Methodology
We extracted 66 linguistic features from the same French register corpus using both parsers, then performed parallel MDAs to test whether the dimensional structure is stable across NLP pipelines.

Loading the Data
Both feature sets were pre-computed and normalized to counts per 1,000 words. The feature extraction workflow is shown below (not executed during rendering):

# Feature extraction workflow (not executed - data loaded from saved files)
fr_features_spacy <- biber(fr_spacy_parsed, measure = "none", normalize = TRUE) |>
  left_join(french_register_corpus |> select(doc_id, register), by = "doc_id") |>
  select(doc_id, register, everything()) |>
  mutate(register = as.factor(register))

fr_features_ud <- biber(fr_udpipe_parsed, measure = "none", normalize = TRUE) |>
  left_join(french_register_corpus |> select(doc_id, register), by = "doc_id") |>
  select(doc_id, register, everything()) |>
  mutate(register = as.factor(register))

We load the pre-extracted features from both parsers:

library(pseudobibeR.fr)
library(mda.biber)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(gt)
library(broom)
library(gtsummary)
library(tibble)

# Load pre-extracted features from both parsers
load("../data/fr_features_spacy.rda")
load("../data/fr_features_ud.rda")

# Remove twitter due to extreme brevity (mean 22 words/doc)
fr_features_spacy <- fr_features_spacy %>% filter(register != "twitter")
fr_features_ud <- fr_features_ud %>% filter(register != "twitter")

cat("spaCy corpus:", nrow(fr_features_spacy), "documents\n")

spaCy corpus: 2113 documents

cat("UDPipe corpus:", nrow(fr_features_ud), "documents\n")

UDPipe corpus: 2113 documents








NoteData Availability



This vignette requires pre-computed feature data files. If youŌĆÖre rendering locally without these files, the code chunks will not execute but are shown for reference.

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Scree Plots: Determining Dimensionality
The scree plots show the proportion of variance explained by each factor:
screeplot_mda(fr_features_spacy)
screeplot_mda(fr_features_ud)






spaCy eigenvalues







UDPipe eigenvalues





Both parsers suggest a 3-factor solution with a clear elbow at Factor 4. The eigenvalue profiles are nearly identical, indicating stable dimensionality.

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Factor Loadings Comparison
We extract 3 factors using a minimum correlation of 0.2 and loading threshold of 0.35:

loadings_spacy <- mda_loadings(
  obs_by_group = fr_features_spacy,
  n_factors = 3,
  cor_min = 0.2,
  threshold = 0.35
)

loadings_ud <- mda_loadings(
  obs_by_group = fr_features_ud,

Interpreting the Loadings
Both parsers reveal remarkably consistent factor structures:
Factor 1 represents an Interactional vs.┬ĀInformation Production dimension. The positive pole is characterized by:

Pronouns: Second person (spaCy: 0.83, UDPipe: 0.73), first person (spaCy: 0.78, UDPipe: 0.59), third person (UDPipe: 0.69)
Interactive features: WH-questions (0.56/0.54), discourse particles (0.44/0.51), clausal coordination (0.58/0.55)
Deixis: Demonstrative pronouns (spaCy: 0.74), indefinite pronouns (0.41/0.47)

The negative pole features:

Nominal style: Other nouns (ŌłÆ0.86/ŌłÆ0.63), nominalizations (ŌłÆ0.49/ŌłÆ0.50)
Structural complexity: Prepositions (ŌłÆ0.51/ŌłÆ0.48), mean word length (ŌłÆ0.33/ŌłÆ0.33)
Non-finite forms: Phrasal coordination (ŌłÆ0.19/ŌłÆ0.20), past participle whiz-deletions (ŌłÆ0.21/ŌłÆ0.19)

Factor 2 shows explicit stance and subordination features with high loadings on adverbs (0.64/0.51), subordinators (0.50/0.69), infinitives (UDPipe: 0.66), and complement clauses.
Factor 3 differs slightly between parsers but captures perfect aspect (UDPipe: 0.92) and passive constructions (UDPipe: agentless passives 0.71).
The cross-parser consistency is striking: key features maintain similar loading patterns, and the fundamental Interactional vs.┬ĀInformational contrast emerges identically from both NLP pipelines.

Regression Models
Linear regression confirms strong register effects with both parsers. The UDPipe model achieves R┬▓ = 0.715 (Adjusted R┬▓ = 0.714), slightly higher than the spaCy modelŌĆÖs R┬▓ = 0.703 (Adjusted R┬▓ = 0.702). Both models show highly significant F-statistics (p < 0.001), indicating that register explains approximately 70% of the variance in Factor 1 scores.
The coefficient patterns are nearly identical across parsers, with spoken and fiction registers at the positive (interactional) pole and academic, news, and Wikipedia at the negative (informational) pole. This cross-parser consistency validates the robustness of the dimensional structure.

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Cross-Linguistic Alignment

BiberŌĆÖs Dimension 1 (English)
BiberŌĆÖs (1988) Dimension 1 contrasts:
Positive pole (Involved/Interactional): - Private verbs (think, feel) - First/second person pronouns - Contractions - Present tense - Discourse particles
Negative pole (Informational): - Nouns - Long words - Prepositions - Attributive adjectives - Past participles


French Factor 1
Our French analysis replicates this fundamental distinction:
Positive pole (Interactional): - Spoken and fiction registers (dialogue) - First/second person pronouns - Contractions (pŌĆÖtit, mŌĆÖsieur) - Present tense - ŌĆ£QueŌĆØ deletion (informal complementizer omission)
Negative pole (Informational): - Academic, news, and Wikipedia registers - Nominalizations - Prepositions - Attributive adjectives - Longer words

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Key Implementation Detail: Contraction Detection
A critical validation was ensuring that the contraction detection (f_59_contractions) worked consistently across parsers. French distinguishes:

Grammatical elisions (mandatory): lŌĆÖ (le), dŌĆÖ (de), quŌĆÖ (que) - articles, prepositions
True contractions (informal): pŌĆÖtit (petit), mŌĆÖsieur (monsieur) - lexical words

We implemented POS-based filtering:
# Exclude tokens with grammatical POS tags
elision_pos_tags <- c("DET", "ADP", "PRON", "ADV", "SCONJ")
contractions <- apostrophe_tokens %>%
  filter(!pos %in% elision_pos_tags)
Both spaCy and UDPipe assign identical POS tags to elisions, ensuring consistent counts:

spaCy: lŌĆÖ (DET), dŌĆÖ (ADP), sŌĆÖ (PRON), nŌĆÖ (ADV), quŌĆÖ (SCONJ)
UDPipe: lŌĆÖ (DET), dŌĆÖ (ADP), sŌĆÖ (PRON), nŌĆÖ (ADV), quŌĆÖ (SCONJ)

Both parsers correctly tag true contractions with lexical POS (ADJ, NOUN, PROPN), allowing them to pass the filter.

title: Cross-Parser Validation of French Register Dimensions
text:
Conclusions

Parser robustness: spaCy and UDPipe produce nearly identical MDA results, validating the feature extraction implementation
Cross-linguistic universality: French Factor 1 aligns with BiberŌĆÖs English Dimension 1, supporting the theory that Interactional vs.┬ĀInformation Production is a fundamental axis of register variation
Implementation reliability: POS-based feature detection (especially for contractions) works consistently across NLP pipelines
Methodological transparency: The pseudobibeR.fr package enables reproducible register analysis with multiple parser backends

title: Welcome
text:
pseudobibeR.fr extracts Douglas BiberŌĆÖs multidimensional features from French texts that have already been tagged and dependency-parsed. The package mirrors the English-centric pseudobiber, but swaps in French heuristics, dictionaries, and edge cases created by researchers at Paris 8 University, as well as discovered while working with both the Chambers & Le BaronŌĆÖs academic corpus and a purpose-built corpus of spoken French, French tweets, and French fiction.



Purpose-built heuristics. Feature detectors borrow from BiberŌĆÖs cross-linguistic descriptions but encode French-specific cues (e.g. relative pronouns, modal periphrases, stranded prepositions). See the complete list of 67 features with French equivalents and examples.
Dataset-backed validation. Each heuristic is illustrated inside the packageŌĆÖs french_examples dataset and mirrored in tests/testthat, so you can inspect concrete sentences while refining your own corpora.
Tidy, opinionated output. Results are returned as one row per document with both raw counts and optional normalized rates, ready for downstream modeling or visualization.





Parse your corpus with either spaCy (via spacyr) or udpipe. Ensure you request POS tags, morphological features, and dependency arcs.
Call biber(tokens) to compute 67 feature families plus lexical richness metrics.
Join any project metadata, normalize counts with normalize_counts(), and visualize/register-space positions as needed.




New to the package? Start with the Getting Started vignette for a complete workflow walkthrough. For detailed examples with real corpora, see:

Cross-Parser Validation - Demonstrates MDA with multi-register French corpus
ChambersŌĆōLe Baron Walkthrough - Analyzes disciplinary variation in academic French

title: Exploring the ChambersŌĆōLe Baron corpus
text:
Parsing the corpus (documented pipeline)
We provide pre-parsed outputs for reproducibility, but the pipeline below illustrates how to regenerate them from the raw text.

library(udpipe)
model <- udpipe_download_model(language = "french-gsd")
ud_model <- udpipe_load_model(model$file_model)

fc_udpipe_parsed <- udpipe_annotate(
  ud_model,
  x = french_corpus$text,
  doc_id = french_corpus$doc_id,
  tagger = "default",
  parser = "default"
)

save(fc_udpipe_parsed, file = "../data/fc_udpipe_parsed.rda")

A parallel recipe using spaCy is already stored in _quarto/data/fc_spacy_parsed.rda.

title: Exploring the ChambersŌĆōLe Baron corpus
text:
Compute Biber features

features_ud <- biber(fc_udpipe_parsed, measure = "none", normalize = TRUE) |>
  left_join(french_corpus |> select(doc_id, discipline), by = "doc_id") |>
  select(doc_id, discipline, everything()) |>
  mutate(discipline = as.factor(discipline))

Factor 1 Interpretation
Factor 1 closely resembles BiberŌĆÖs classic Dimension 1: Involved vs.┬ĀInformational Production.
Features with positive loadings (>0.20) indicate more involved, interactive discourse:

Demonstrative pronouns (0.61), be as main verb (0.60), and adverbs (0.58)
Conditional clauses with if (0.56) and third-person pronouns (0.54)
Discourse particles (0.48) and analytic negation (0.42)

Features with negative loadings (<ŌłÆ0.20) indicate more informational, edited prose:

Nominalizations (ŌłÆ0.62) and mean word length (ŌłÆ0.62)
Prepositions (ŌłÆ0.53) and gerunds (ŌłÆ0.39)
Past and present participial whiz deletions (ŌłÆ0.36, ŌłÆ0.33)
Attributive adjectives (ŌłÆ0.26) and phrasal coordination (ŌłÆ0.25)

This dimension captures the fundamental distinction between texts that engage readers directly through personal reference and simplified syntax (positive pole) versus texts that convey dense information through abstract nominalization and complex modification (negative pole).

heatmap_mda(loadings, n_factor = 1)

Key findings:

Philosophy scores highest on Factor 1, showing a remarkably involved, interactive styleŌĆöunusual for academic writing. Philosophy articles in this corpus employ more demonstratives, conditionals, and discourse markers than other disciplines, suggesting a more dialogic engagement with arguments and counterarguments.
Sciences (Biology, Medicine, Physics) cluster at the negative pole, favoring dense nominalization and complex noun phrases typical of empirical research reporting.
Social sciences (Economics, Linguistics) occupy intermediate positions, balancing technical precision with explanatory discourse.

This pattern suggests that even within formal academic French, disciplinary conventions shape fundamental choices about how to structure information and engage readers. PhilosophyŌĆÖs outlier status highlights the disciplineŌĆÖs tradition of argument-driven prose that maintains traces of oral disputation.
Importantly, this finding replicates cross-linguistically. Hardy and R├ČmerŌĆÖs (2013) multidimensional analysis of the MICUSP corpus (English student writing) similarly identified Philosophy at the extreme end of what they termed ŌĆ£Involved, Academic NarrativeŌĆØŌĆöcharacterized by high use of personal pronouns, present tense verbs, and stance markers. The convergence across French and English academic corpora suggests that PhilosophyŌĆÖs distinctive rhetorical conventions transcend language-specific constraints, reflecting deeper epistemological commitments to dialogic argumentation.

Register Details

Academic (159 documents)
Subset of the ChambersŌĆōLe Baron corpus representing formal academic prose across multiple disciplines.


Fiction (46 documents)
Sampling method: Line-based sampling from 100 French literary texts

Extracted 150-line chunks from diverse fiction sources
Ensures variety across authors and periods
Avoids over-representation from single long novels



News (503 documents)
Source: Chambers-Rostand Corpus of Journalistic French
Contemporary French journalism covering various topics and publication outlets.


Spoken (87 documents)
Source: French Oral Narrative Corpus
Transcribed oral narratives representing conversational French. Includes features typical of spoken interaction: filled pauses, discourse particles, informal contractions.


Twitter (7,197 documents)
Note: Excluded from multidimensional analyses due to extreme brevity (mean 22 words/document), which creates normalization artifacts when computing features per 1,000 words.
French tweets collected to represent informal digital communication.


Wikipedia (1,318 documents)
French Wikipedia articles representing informational but accessible encyclopedic prose. More formal than conversational registers but less technical than academic writing.

title: Data Sources
text:
Data Files
All corpora are stored in data-raw/corpus-data/ in two formats:
Parquet format (recommended for large datasets):
french_corpus.parquet             # ChambersŌĆōLe Baron corpus
french_register_corpus.parquet    # Multi-register corpus
RDA format (R-native):
french_corpus.rda
french_register_corpus.rda
Pre-parsed versions for documentation rendering (in _quarto/data/):
fc_udpipe_parsed.rda    # ChambersŌĆōLe Baron parsed with UDPipe
fc_spacy_parsed.rda     # ChambersŌĆōLe Baron parsed with spaCy
fr_features_ud.rda      # Extracted features (UDPipe)
fr_features_spacy.rda   # Extracted features (spaCy)

title: Data Sources
text:
Usage in Vignettes

Getting Started
Uses synthetic examples to demonstrate basic workflow without requiring corpus data.


Cross-Parser Validation
Uses the French Register Corpus (excluding Twitter) to compare UDPipe and spaCy parsers across 5 registers: academic, fiction, news, spoken, wikipedia.


ChambersŌĆōLe Baron Walkthrough
Uses the ChambersŌĆōLe Baron Corpus to analyze disciplinary variation in French academic writing.

title: Data Sources
text:
Accessing the Data
The corpora are not distributed with the package due to size and licensing considerations. They are used for documentation and validation purposes, with pre-computed feature extractions stored in _quarto/data/ for vignette rendering.
For researchers interested in replicating analyses:

Download corpora from Oxford Text Archive (links above)
Place raw text files in data-raw/corpus-data/
Use parsing scripts in data-raw/ to regenerate parsed versions
Extract features using biber() function
